title: Quadlet Pod option does not expand systemd specifiers
body:
### Issue Description

With the below files, `Pod=` results in a `quadlet-generator` error.

`test.pod`:
```conf
[Pod]
Podname=%N
```

`test.container`
```conf
[Container]
Pod=%N.pod
Image=docker.io/alpine
```


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create `test.pod` and `test.container` in `/etc/containers/systemd` as above
2. Run `systemctl daemon-reload`



### Describe the results you received

`quadlet-generator[...]: converting "test.container": quadlet pod unit %N.pod does not exist`

### Describe the results you expected

A successful quadlet should have been generated, with the same values as if `Pod=test.pod` was used.

### podman info output

```yaml
Podman version `5.8.3` on Fedora 44.
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
While I understand the issue and agree that Quadlet fails to resolve the reference, I'm not sure I understand the real life use case. In order to use the `%N`, the `.container` and `.pod` files must have the same name (as you showed in your example with `test`). Therefore, it only works for single container pods which I'm not sure their benefit in Podman.

Can you please elaborate on your actual use case?

The container in my case uses `ContainerName=%N`, so I simply figured that `Pod=%N.pod` fit well and got confused about the error, which is inconsistent with the usage of specifiers elsewhere in the quadlet definition.

I do have another (quadlet-defined) container in the pod, which indeed has to replace the `%N` with the actual name; this limits the usefulness in terms of renaming of the quadlet files (only one less value to replace), but it still retains an arguable readability improvement for the one container.

I agree that it's a minor issue, of course.

While we try to support the different specifiers, Quadlet does have issues using them as references to other units. Keep in mind that `%N` resolves to something different in each service (being the name of the service). So, using it to reference other units may lead to issues.
I still wonder, why do you need to wrap a single container in a pod. In my view it just adds the complexity of managing two files, their linking (which result in this issue), having to know which key goes where (e.g. `PublishPort` has to go to the pod and not the container). Where is the benefit?

> I still wonder, why do you need to wrap a single container in a pod

I do currently have 2 containers in the pod, with just one of them sharing the same name as the pod. Still, `Pod=%N.pod` should ideally work for that container, even though it won't work for the other container(s) in the pod.

> Where is the benefit?

Even before adding the second container, I found having the pod useful in this case as I needed the following execution order:

1. Podman network namespace creation
2. Host-side script that creates a wireguard interface and [moves it into the above netns](https://www.wireguard.com/netns/#ordinary-containerization)
3. Container startup that depends on the interface from (2)
